name: Quechua

formality.description: Quechua does not have a grammaticalized T-V distinction. There is a single second-person pronoun (qam). Formality and respect are expressed through vocabulary choice, use of honorific suffixes (e.g., -lla for softening), and cultural context rather than pronoun alternation. Software UI uses standard, clear Quechua.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Quechua has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronouns (pay) are gender-neutral. No special inclusive conventions are needed.

professional.label: Allin Runasimi (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and institutional communication. Based on Southern Quechua (Cusco-Collao variety).
professional.prompt: Professional Quechua (Runasimi), Southern/Cusco-Collao variety. Clear, accessible language suitable for software interfaces. Use the standardized orthography with the five-vowel system (a, e, i, o, u). Include appropriate evidential suffixes — use -mi/-n for direct knowledge, -si/-s for reported information. Prefer pan-Quechua vocabulary where possible over highly local dialectal terms.

notes: Quechua is a macrolanguage (ISO 639-3 'que') comprising a family of related languages. This card represents Southern Quechua (Cusco-Collao variety), the most widely spoken and digitally resourced variety. Other major varieties include Ayacucho Quechua (quy), Ecuadorian Kichwa (qug), and Bolivian Quechua (quh). An orthography debate exists between three-vowel (a, i, u) and five-vowel (a, e, i, o, u) systems — this card uses the five-vowel system commonly used in Peru.
